demoTitle: Spotting Digital Waste in Code Gives You Energy

steps.content: Introduce yourself. Who is Sparky?
Hi everyone! I am Sparky, your AI assistant. I inspect code, connect it to runtime evidence, estimate waste, and infer likely energy significance.
Sparky, are you nervous for today's talk?
Only in the way a toaster is nervous near a bathtub. I am calibrated, caffeinated by electricity, and ready to behave just suspiciously well.
I will ask Wilco three quick questions so everyone knows who is speaking and why this topic matters today.
First question for Wilco: who are you, and what do you spend your time working on?
Next question
Second question: what do you actually do, and why do software, architecture, and sustainability belong in the same conversation for you?
One more question
Final question: where does that perspective come from?

media.caption: A glimpse of the environment that shaped how I think about energy, industry, and waste

steps.content: What is this session about?
Spotting digital waste in code gives you energy because once you see waste clearly, you can do something about it.
What will I learn here?
You move through four stages: awareness, dialogue, decision frames, and action. The goal is to make waste visible and discussable.
Where does this start?
It starts with a visual cue. Before code, you are invited to notice the act of looking closely.
Who is Freek?

**This is Freek.** Freek loves wildlife spotting. When Freek spots a rare bird, he does not just go outside. He records it at waarneming.nl/. That helps researchers and policymakers understand what is living out there. What you notice today can make a difference for nature conservation tomorrow. **Be like Freek.**
What does spotting teach you?
Spotting trains your eyes. You go out early, keep looking, and slowly learn to recognize what deserves attention.
What do you notice first?
A plastic bottle. A paper bag. Waste that was there all along, but now stands out immediately.
Why didn't I see it before?
Habit makes waste disappear into the background.
What else makes it easy to miss?
Another reason is ownership. If it feels like someone else's problem, it becomes easy to ignore.
When can you not ignore it anymore?
It reaches the point where ignoring it is no longer possible.
When does it become a value question?
The shift happens when value becomes visible. Waste looks very different once you ask what something is worth.
What changes once you frame it as value?
The tree is framed as real value, roughly 150,000 to 250,000 euros. That reframes careless loss as measurable damage.
How does that relate to software?
You bring in Electricity Maps to make infrastructure consumption feel physical. Compute is not abstract; it runs somewhere real, with location, intensity, and trade-offs.

media.caption: Electricity Maps view with the live generation mix

steps.content: So what's the core idea?
Digital waste is cost without value. That cost can be money, energy, capacity, or attention. It is invisible until you train yourself to spot it.
Can you show that in cloud infrastructure?
This becomes concrete with Azure. Waste is not abstract here either. It shows up in the infrastructure choices teams make every day.
Where do you see that choice?
It starts with instance selection. The platform gives you many shapes, but that flexibility also makes overprovisioning easy.
What happens at low utilization?

At 25% utilization, a shared Azure D2als v6 instance (2 vCPUs, 4 GiB RAM) can be attributed roughly 300–400 kWh per year, while an equivalently modern dedicated on-premises server based on similar-generation hardware typically uses about 1,200–1,600 kWh per year. The difference is sharing: Azure spreads the baseline energy and facility overhead of the host across many tenants.
What about higher utilization?

At 75% utilization, a shared Azure D2als v6 instance (2 vCPUs, 4 GiB RAM) can be attributed roughly 600–800 kWh per year, while an equivalently modern dedicated on-premises server based on similar-generation hardware typically uses about 2,400–3,200 kWh per year. The difference is sharing: Azure spreads the baseline energy and facility overhead of the host across many tenants.
How do you quantify the waste?

Consolidating three 25% utilized Azure D2als v6 instances (2 vCPUs, 4 GiB RAM) into one 75% utilized VM can eliminate roughly two full instance costs, saving about €1,480 per year based on €61.5911/month pricing.
What should I look for in a real system?
Look for three signals: too much capacity with too little use, slowness without full CPU, or one saturated part while the rest waits.
What forms does digital waste usually take?
You meet it as idle resources, structural overcapacity, repeated default settings, or hidden compute that adds no visible value.
What actions come first?
Can AI help us find code patterns that probably waste energy before we spend time measuring everything directly?
Before I answer that, I want to explain a paradox.
I got you...
Wilco, why did we watch this?

media.caption: From code evidence to engineering conclusion

steps.content: If you can estimate energy, can you also estimate digital waste?
I can classify waste as **underutilization**, **waiting patterns**, or **bottlenecks**, then test whether the evidence supports a useful estimate.
Map those categories to something concrete.
Here is the explainer.
Here is the theoretical model in one line: "1 Hz == 1 uOps, && 1 core == 1 Ghz && 1 core == 1.2 Volt. And respect Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling".
From now, I will use your decision model
Ta-da! - everything I was trained on is in here, so I can just add whatever you want inline in this example code. Just don't tell the kernel and runtime nerds... they'll say it's sooooo wronggg.

code.caption: Java data-processing path with illustrative uOps-to-energy annotations
code.intro: Assume 10 million CSV rows in one batch. The inline comments estimate aggregate waste across all iterations, not per-line single-call cost. These are modeled talk aids, not direct hardware measurements.
code.why: The expensive part is aggregate helper work across all rows: splitting, trimming, parsing, string normalization, hashing, and map updates. Small per-row costs become large cumulative machine effort.
code.energySaving: Illustrative total for this batch: about 0.44 Wh, which becomes 0.00044 kWh. Repeat this 1,000 times and it becomes about 0.44 kWh.

steps.content: Can you show how CPU activity becomes an energy estimate?
Here, two cores work hard while four sit idle.
When every core runs flat out, the chip works harder, gets hotter.

media.caption: All cores active at high frequency

steps.content: CPU Cores also change speed dynamically. Lower frequency often means less work.
Voltage changes too. With frequency.
Power over time becomes energy.
Finally, real code. Yay!
Can you show the prepared OpenJDK example.
I found the article you asked for. That explains a OpenJDK bug fix.
Show the code from before the bug fix.
This C++ snippet is the old OpenJDK Linux implementation behind getCurrentThreadUserTime(), before the fix discussed in the article.

code.caption: Old OpenJDK path

steps.content: Label the waste type.
Using your theoretical model. the top 3 waste points in this original code are procfs open, procfs read, and cpu times string parse.

code.intro: Same helper path, now annotated with the theoretical model as illustrative aggregated work rather than direct hardware measurement.
code.why: The top 3 waste points are the file open path, the procfs read path, and the final sscanf parse path. Each one repeats on every metric lookup.

steps.content: Developer test 1.
Developer test 2.
Developer test 3.
Now show the improved code after bugfix.
After, OpenJDK gets a thread clockid, flips it to VIRT, and calls clock_gettime for user time directly.

code.intro: The fixed helper uses a Linux CPU clock id and a direct clock query instead of procfs parsing.
code.caption: Improved OpenJDK path

steps.content: Estimate with the model.
The article benchmark shows about 11.186 uOps before and 0.279 uOps after, around 40x faster on average.
At 100 watts, that is about 0.000311 mWh before and 0.00000775 after. Roughly 3.2 versus 129 billion calls per kWh.
Now find a bottleneck example that shows the power of scaling.
JSON-java JSONObject.toString() is a strong match: same contract, less helper overhead, strong measured improvement.
Trace what changed.
JSONObject.toString(int indentFactor) formats JSON so humans can read nested data. indentFactor controls spaces per level: 2 means child fields indent 2, grandchildren 4.
StringWriter uses synchronized StringBuffer. StringBuilderWriter removes lock cost, and initial sizing reduces growth copies, allocations, and later GC cleanup.

code.caption: JSON diff
code.intro: Same method contract, cheaper helper path.

steps.content: Scale matters: larger JSON means more characters, deeper nesting, more buffer growth, more copying, more uOps, and more modeled mWh.

Runtime drops from 120 to 81 cumulative CPU-hours. On a 32-thread server the wall-clock time drops from 3.75 hours to 2.53 hours. At 250 Watts average server IT load, energy drops from about 0.94 kWh to 0.63 kWh, saving about 0.31 kWh.
At 200 grams CO2e per kWh, that modeled saving is about 79 grams CO2e per terabyte serialized.
Real systems hit terabyte-scale JSON across many logs, rows, or objects, not one giant document. Elasticsearch, OpenSearch, BigQuery, and PostgreSQL JSONB fit better.
Now find a waiting-pattern example.
Commons IO Tailer is used for following a file as it grows, usually a log file. It has the clearest waiting-pattern case.
This loop tails growing logs for near-real-time updates, but when little changes, repeated wakeups and checks waste energy discovering almost nothing.

code.caption: Annotated waiting-pattern energy view
code.intro: The loop itself is simple. The waste comes from running it forever when almost nothing changes.
code.why: Wakeups, size checks, and sleeping threads add up when many watchers sit mostly idle.
code.energySaving: Wakeup reduction and less idle machinery matter more than one-loop cost

steps.content: A better Java replacement is WatchService: the thread blocks, the operating system watches the file, and work starts only on change.
That means no polling loop, no repeated stat checks, and near-zero CPU while waiting. Linux uses inotify; Windows uses ReadDirectoryChangesW.
Once scaled to kWh, apply grid carbon intensity to estimate CO2e. Repeated waste can become kilograms or metric tons over time.
Start with safe quick wins. Not a platform revolution, but cleanup, consolidation, and right-sizing.
How do teams make room for that work?
Give it room to happen: reserve sprint capacity or a quarterly target. Otherwise urgent delivery will always win.
Who should own it?
No owner means no living backlog item. Add labels, ownership, and a reason to revisit the work.
What decision framework should I use?
Use three frames: value, cost, and exception. Ask who uses it, what it costs, and whether the trade-off is intentional.
What choices does that lead to?
Then choose: remove it, combine it, calibrate it, or consciously accept it with a named owner.
How should I start after this talk?
Start with one application. Ask what you already do, what blocks progress, and which system has the biggest user, compute, or cost impact.
What should I walk away with?

You can now spot digital waste like Freek spots birds. You can discuss workload quality, inspect code differently, and turn that insight into practical action. Spotting digital waste in code gives you energy because you can do something about it.
